signals.idea: Video editor timeline: well-engineered redesign. Core problems: (1) Two competing drag systems (pointer-based useClipDrag + HTML5 useTimelineEditing) each imperatively creating DOM indicator elements — unify into one React-managed indicator system. (2) Timeline library (@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor) is half-used — we capture its pointer events before it sees them, override its move callbacks, but still wire its onActionMove* callbacks through context as dead weight. Either fully own clip movement or fully delegate to the library. (3) Drop indicators are imperative DOM manipulation (createElement, document.body.appendChild) in two places — should be a single React portal component. (4) useTimelineEditing.ts is 947 lines mixing 6 concerns. (5) ActionDragState is a shared mutable ref threaded through 5 files with no coordination. (6) EditorPaneTab has a local height hack that belongs in getEffectivePaneHeights. Goal: a system where there's one clear owner for each concern, indicators are declarative React, the library is either used properly or abstracted away, and the code is obvious to read.

unresolved_flags.concern: The proposed `useDragIndicatorState` in `TimelineEditorComponent` would put drag-frame state on the parent that renders `<Timeline>`, so internal clip drags would drive full editor re-renders at pointer-move frequency.

The plan only unifies the five portal elements, but it leaves the separate `drop-target-new-track` affordance unaccounted for, so indicator ownership would still stay split across both drag implementations.

Step 7 misidentifies `onChange` as the same-row move path after removing `onActionMove*`. In this repo, library `onChange` only happens when the library drag lifecycle runs, while `useClipDrag` already persists same-row moves itself.

Step 7 does not actually establish a single owner for clip movement. Removing `onActionMove*` while keeping the library draggable leaves `@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor` free to mutate action positions and drive `onChange`, so the library and `useClipDrag` would still compete.

The new indicator state is placed too high in the tree. `useDragIndicatorState()` in `TimelineEditor.tsx` would rerender the full timeline editor on every drag update, which is a likely performance regression from the current imperative DOM path.

signals.loop_summary: Iteration 1. Weighted score trajectory: 7.5. Plan deltas: n/a. Recurring critiques: 0. Resolved flags: 0. Open significant flags: 5.

items: Only one indicator system exists — the <DropIndicator> React portal component — used by both internal clip drag and external HTML5 drag-drop
No imperative document.createElement or document.body.appendChild calls for drop indicators remain anywhere in the codebase
actionDragStateRef is internal to useClipDrag, not threaded through context or shared across files
useTimelineEditing.ts no longer exists; its concerns are split into useClipEditing (~250 LOC), useExternalDrop, useAssetManagement, and useClipResize
The @xzdarcy library's onActionMove* callbacks are either removed (if Step 6 confirms they're unnecessary) or clearly documented as required coordination points
EditorPaneTab has no local height calculation — all height logic lives in getEffectivePaneHeights
All existing user interactions work: clip drag (horizontal + cross-track), clip resize, external file/generation/asset drop, split, delete, mute, text add

unresolved_flags.concern: The proposed `useDragIndicatorState` in `TimelineEditorComponent` would put drag-frame state on the parent that renders `<Timeline>`, so internal clip drags would drive full editor re-renders at pointer-move frequency.
unresolved_flags.evidence: `useClipDrag` updates drop state on every pointer move today (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:307-350`). `TimelineEditorComponent` is the parent of the full track list and `<Timeline>` tree (`src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx:159-208`). Replacing the imperative updates with `useState` there, as described in Steps 2-3, would make indicator movement re-render that whole subtree unless the state is isolated from the editor shell.

unresolved_flags.concern: The plan only unifies the five portal elements, but it leaves the separate `drop-target-new-track` affordance unaccounted for, so indicator ownership would still stay split across both drag implementations.
unresolved_flags.evidence: Both drag paths currently toggle the new-track banner imperatively via `editAreaEl.classList.add/remove('drop-target-new-track')` in `src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:129,225-231` and `src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineEditing.ts:586-592,651-663`. Step 1 defines `<DropIndicator>` around the appended row/line/ghost/label elements only, and Steps 4-5 only replace those imperative indicator helpers.

unresolved_flags.concern: Step 7 misidentifies `onChange` as the same-row move path after removing `onActionMove*`. In this repo, library `onChange` only happens when the library drag lifecycle runs, while `useClipDrag` already persists same-row moves itself.
unresolved_flags.evidence: The library calls `onChange` from `handleEditorDataChange` only after its own drag end mutates editor data (`node_modules/@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor/src/components/edit_area/edit_action.tsx:121-135`, `node_modules/@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor/src/components/timeline.tsx:80-86`). Separately, `useClipDrag` already commits same-row moves with `moveClipToRow(...)` on pointer up (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:375-382`). So keeping `onChange` because it is 'used for same-row horizontal moves' is not aligned with the actual code path.

unresolved_flags.concern: Step 7 does not actually establish a single owner for clip movement. Removing `onActionMove*` while keeping the library draggable leaves `@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor` free to mutate action positions and drive `onChange`, so the library and `useClipDrag` would still compete.
unresolved_flags.evidence: `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx` still renders `<Timeline>` in draggable mode and the plan does not disable library dragging. In `node_modules/@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor/src/components/edit_area/edit_action.tsx`, `handleDragEnd` updates `action.start/end` and calls `setEditorData(editorData)` even if no `onActionMoveStart/onActionMoving/onActionMoveEnd` callbacks are supplied; those callbacks are optional glue, not the drag switch. The current custom path also captures on `pointerdown`/`setPointerCapture` in `src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts`, but the library still uses `onMouseDown` on its drag surface (`edit_action.tsx`), so Step 6/7 needs an explicit ownership mechanism, not just callback removal.

unresolved_flags.concern: The new indicator state is placed too high in the tree. `useDragIndicatorState()` in `TimelineEditor.tsx` would rerender the full timeline editor on every drag update, which is a likely performance regression from the current imperative DOM path.
unresolved_flags.evidence: Step 3 mounts indicator state in `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx`, the same component that renders `<Timeline>`, track labels, `getActionRender`, and thumbnail maps. Step 4 then drives updates from `useClipDrag` on every `pointermove`; that hook currently has no RAF throttling for internal drags (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts`). Today both indicator systems avoid React rerenders by mutating DOM directly. Moving that hot-path state into the parent timeline component is likely to churn the whole editor during drag.
